planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Maru is a pollination-variant astringent (PVA) persimmon — when unpollinated, the fruit remains astringent until fully soft; when cross-pollinated, the seeds stimulate the flesh to develop dark-colored, non-astringent regions (jelly-like, very sweet spots) even before the fruit softens. Plant with a male persimmon or another D. kaki cultivar within 50 feet for full PVA expression.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Maru is a Japanese persimmon cultivar that exemplifies the PVA (pollination-variant astringent) type — uniquely, the flavor and texture of its fruit changes dramatically based on whether the flower was pollinated. Unpollinated Maru fruits are uniformly orange-colored and astringent until fully ripe-soft; pollinated fruits develop dark brown or black spots and streaks in the flesh (called 'chocolate flesh') around the seeds, and these dark regions are sweet, jelly-like, and completely non-astringent while still firm — a prized texture and flavor combination. Fully pollinated Maru fruits may have most of the flesh dark-colored and sweet, making them among the most interesting persimmon-eating experiences available. In zone 9 Maru produces reliable crops of medium-sized, round to slightly oblate fruits ripening in October–November. The dark flesh and sweet, pudding-like texture of cross-pollinated fruits makes Maru an outstanding garden and fresh-market variety.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Asian Persimmon Variety Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Persimmon for Florida
California Rare Fruit Growers — PVA Persimmon Varieties

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Coffeecake is a PVA (pollination-variant astringent) persimmon — cross-pollination from a male persimmon or another D. kaki cultivar within 50 feet causes the flesh to develop rich, dark cinnamon-brown coloration and complex, non-astringent sweetness even before the fruit softens. Without pollination the fruit remains astringent until fully soft-ripe.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Coffeecake is a PVA (pollination-variant astringent) Japanese persimmon selected for its distinctive deep cinnamon-brown flesh coloration and extraordinary flavor when cross-pollinated — the richly colored flesh has a complex, spiced sweetness reminiscent of warm cinnamon and brown sugar that gives the variety its distinctive name. The medium to large, flat-round, orange-skinned fruits develop their characteristic dark interior progressively as pollination stimulates dark flesh development, and fully pollinated fruits may have almost entirely dark, non-astringent, sweet, dense flesh while still firm enough to slice. The flavor of cross-pollinated Coffeecake is considered among the finest of any persimmon type — complex, sweet, and aromatic with none of the astringency that makes unripe persimmons inedible. In zone 9 Coffeecake ripens in late October–November and the tree is vigorous and productive with attractive form and good fall leaf color.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — PVA Persimmon Cultivar Guide
California Rare Fruit Growers — Coffeecake Persimmon
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Persimmon for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Nazemetz is partially self-fertile but produces significantly heavier crops when cross-pollinated with another feijoa cultivar such as Coolidge, Apollo, or Mammoth. In zone 9 feijoa flowers and fruits reliably; harvest fruits by collecting those that fall naturally from the shrub October–December. Fruits must be harvested promptly when dropped as they do not store well at room temperature.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Nazemetz is a California-selected feijoa (pineapple guava) cultivar prized for producing the largest fruits of any commonly available named variety — round to slightly pear-shaped fruits typically reaching 4–8 ounces, significantly larger than the 2–4 ounce fruits of unnamed seedling plants. The rich, complex, aromatic flavor combines pineapple, mint, guava, and strawberry notes into a unique tropical flavor that is highly distinctive and unlike any other common fruit. Feijoa (Acca sellowiana) is a South American fruit increasingly popular in California, New Zealand, and the Gulf South for its exceptional zone 8–10 adaptability, drought tolerance, and ornamental value — the spring flowers with white petals and red stamens are edible and delicious eaten directly from the plant as a sweet-spicy spring treat. In zone 9 Nazemetz produces fruit reliably with minimal care; the fruit drops naturally when ripe and is harvested by collecting fallen fruits.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers — Feijoa Variety Guide
University of California Cooperative Extension — Nazemetz Feijoa
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Feijoa for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant air-layered or container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant March–May. Kohala longan requires a brief cool-dry period (cool temperatures below 59°F for several weeks) to trigger flower induction — this chilling occurs naturally in zone 9 winters, making zone 9 a better longan climate than zone 10. Do not overwater in winter; allow the tree to experience mild stress during the cool season to promote flowering. Young trees are frost-sensitive; protect below 30°F.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Kohala is the most widely planted named longan cultivar in Hawaii and South Florida, selected at the University of Hawaii for consistently large, sweet, reliable fruit production in tropical and subtropical conditions. The large clusters of walnut-sized fruits with tan-brown papery shells and translucent white sweet flesh ripen in August–September — the classic longan flavor is a delicate sweetness with floral notes and a refreshing juicy texture, less intense and more floral than lychee. In zone 9 the mild winter temperatures provide the natural chilling that longan requires to initiate flower buds, making zone 9a an ideal longan climate. Kohala trees are vigorous growers that can eventually reach 30–40 feet in favorable conditions but are maintained smaller by selective pruning. The species produces abundantly once established, with mature trees bearing hundreds of pounds of fruit in a good year.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Kohala Longan Production Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Longan Production in Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Longan and Lychee for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (preferred) or container-grown plants in spring; in zone 9 plant March–May. Red dragon fruit requires a sturdy trellis, fence, or concrete post for its climbing stem — provide a flat-topped support (old telephone pole, concrete post, or T-post with a flat wooden disc) for the sprawling crown of cladodes. In zone 9 protect from temperatures below 28°F; the thick cladodes survive brief cold snaps but repeated freezes will damage the vine. Fruiting begins 1–2 years from cuttings.

directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, sandy, gritty, low-fertility soil — red dragon fruit is a cactus and will rot rapidly in rich, wet, or clay soils; Red Dragon Fruit (Selenicereus costaricensis, formerly Hylocereus costaricensis) is the red-fleshed species of dragon fruit, producing large, oval, deep red-skinned fruits with vivid magenta-red flesh flecked with tiny black seeds. The deep crimson flesh color comes from betacyanin pigments (the same class as beet pigments) that turn smoothies, yogurt, and beverages a dramatic pink-red color. The flavor is mild and sweet with a watermelon-like freshness that is more subtle than the appearance suggests. Red dragon fruit typically ripens 30–35 days after the large, white nocturnal flowers open — the flowers must be pollinated on the night they open (by hand or by nocturnal pollinators) for fruit to set. In zone 9 the long warm season provides ideal conditions for multiple fruiting cycles per year, typically producing 4–6 fruit-set periods from May through November.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dragon Fruit Production in Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pitaya Production for Gulf Coast
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Dragon Fruit Production
